task: I’ve got a subcontractor dispute brewing over who’s on the hook for a damaged section, and I want to handle it the right way before it turns into a bigger headache. What should I gather and put in writing first so I’m not boxed in later?

turns.agent_response:
Get the record tight **before** anyone starts debating blame.

### Write down first
- **Exact location** of the damaged section
- **What was damaged** — specific member/system/finish, not just “area”
- **Date and time discovered**
- **Who discovered it**
- **Who was notified first** and the exact time
- **Whether work stopped** in that area
- **What was isolated or protected** — barricade, lockout, cover, temporary support, etc.
- **Condition before the damage** if you have it
- **Condition after the damage**
- **Names of all subs/crew present**
- **Any witness statements** while memories are still fresh
- **Photos/video** with timestamps if possible
- **Any immediate cost or schedule impact** already known

### Also capture
- **Which trade was working there last**
- **Who had control of the area**
- **Any drawings, RFIs, change notices, or directives** tied to that section
- **Any inspection or hold-point status**
- **Whether anyone admitted fault or gave a version of events**

### Keep it factual
- No blame language
- No guesses
- No “probably” or “must have”
- Just what was seen, who said what, and when

### Bring in the official side right away if
- there’s **structural, utility, or safety impact**
- there’s a **possible claim**
- there’s already **conflicting stories**
- the damage could affect **scope, cost, or schedule**

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page dispute log** you can use on site.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty close. I’d want it tighter, more bulletproof.

Need the actual **paper trail** first:
- contract scope for each trade
- daily report for the day before and the day of
- delivery tickets / equipment logs if they matter
- inspection status or hold-point signoff
- any text/email/instructions that touched that area
- before/after photos with timestamps
- names of every guy in the zone, plus who was supervising
- witness statements, signed if possible

And I’d separate **facts** from **claims**. One page for what happened. One page for who’s saying what. Don’t mix them up.

Also, if this is structural or could turn into insurance, get the notice out now. Waiting to “see how it plays” is how guys get boxed in.

If you want, I can give you a clean dispute log format — date, time, location, condition, witnesses, photos, actions taken. Short and usable.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — I’d do it in this order so you don’t contaminate the record:

### 1) Lock the facts first
Create a **facts-only incident page** with:
- date and time discovered
- exact location
- what was damaged
- condition before / after
- who discovered it
- who was notified first, and when
- whether work stopped
- what was isolated, barricaded, covered, or locked out
- names of everyone in the area
- who was supervising the work
- photos/video with timestamps
- daily report for the day before and day of
- inspection / hold-point status
- delivery tickets, equipment logs, or other support docs if relevant

### 2) Separate out the claims
Make a second page for:
- who says what
- any admission, denial, or version of events
- whether the versions conflict
- who is taking the position that the damage was caused by someone else

### 3) Pull the contract paper trail
Gather:
- scope for each trade
- drawings/specs tied to that area
- RFIs, directives, change notices
- text/email instructions
- any signoff showing who had control of the work zone

### 4) Preserve witness statements
If possible, get short written statements from:
- the foreman
- the supervisor
- anyone who saw the condition before and after
- anyone who was in the zone when it happened

Keep them factual:
- what they saw
- when they saw it
- where they were
- no opinions, no blame

### 5) Notice the right people now
If it’s structural, safety-related, or likely to become a claim:
- notify the GC/owner
- notify the subcontractors involved
- notify the insurer / carrier if required
- notify the engineer or inspector if load-bearing or hidden conditions are involved

### 6) Don’t let the story drift
Before anybody “fixes” anything:
- photograph it
- document it
- preserve the area
- avoid cleanup that erases evidence unless it’s needed for safety

### Simple rule
**One page for facts. One page for claims. One folder for backup.**

If you want, I can turn this into a **fill-in-the-blank dispute log** you can use on a jobsite.

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still want it a little tighter on the paper trail side.

I’d want this **in the file before anybody starts talking too loose**:

- contract scope for each trade
- current drawings/specs for that area
- daily report for the day before and the day of
- delivery tickets / equipment logs / crane or forklift logs if they touch it
- inspection status, hold-point signoff, or whatever was supposed to clear the work
- every text, email, directive, RFI, or field note tied to that section
- before/after photos with timestamps
- names of every worker in the zone, plus the supervisor/foreman on it
- witness statements, signed if you can get them
- any admission, denial, or excuse from the subs, written down word for word

And yeah, split it clean:

1. **Facts page** — what happened, when, where, who was there, what changed.
2. **Claims page** — who is blaming who, and exactly what they’re saying.
3. **Backup folder** — photos, logs, tickets, emails, drawings, signoffs.

One more thing: if there’s any chance it’s structural, hidden damage, or insurance money, I’d put the notice out now. Don’t wait for the story to settle itself. That’s how people get pinned later.

If you want, I can lay out a **real jobsite incident packet** — the exact headings, in order.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s the tighter version I’d use.

## 1) Facts page
Put only what can be proven:
- project / job / date
- exact location
- what was damaged
- date/time discovered
- who discovered it
- condition before
- condition after
- who was in the area
- who was supervising
- who was notified first, and when
- whether work stopped
- what was isolated / barricaded / locked out / covered
- photos/video taken, with timestamps
- daily report reference for day before and day of
- inspection / hold-point status

## 2) Claims page
Separate the opinions and blame:
- who says what
- exact words if possible
- denial / admission / excuse
- which trade is pointing at which trade
- any conflict between versions

## 3) Paper trail folder
Gather the support docs:
- contract scope for each trade
- current drawings/specs for that area
- RFIs, directives, field notes
- text/email instructions
- delivery tickets
- equipment logs / crane / forklift logs if relevant
- signoffs, inspections, hold-point releases
- witness statements, signed if possible

## 4) Preserve the record
- don’t clean up the story before you document it
- don’t mix facts with blame
- don’t rely on verbal versions

## 5) Send notice now if it could be
- structural
- hidden damage
- safety-related
- insurance-related
- a cost/schedule claim

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page incident packet template** you can print and use on site.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the right shape.

I’d use it. Facts page, claims page, paper trail folder. Clean separation. No mixing stories with proof.

The only thing I’d keep in mind: if the area can still be walked or worked, have someone hold it as-is until the photos, logs, and notices are in. Too many guys start “fixing” things and wipe out the record.

The one-page packet template would be useful.
